title: CSE 312 Mock Midterm — Variant A
description: Covers HTTP parsing, cookies, authentication, and server security patterns from HW1–HW3.

questions.q: In which of the following situations does buffering NOT apply?
options: When receiving requests containing large file uploads
When receiving multiple WebSocket frames back-to-back
When receiving a GET request with no body
When a user registers with a 1,000,000-character username

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT true about WebSocket frames?
options: The payload can be masked using XOR
They have a maximum payload size on the order of exabytes
They have a variable length that must be parsed
They require bit-level parsing to read
The payload will be masked when sent from the client, but not from the server

questions.q: The WebSocket handshake ensures that:
options: A new UDP connection is created for WebSocket
The server understands the WebSocket protocol
A connection over an unreliable network is established
Nothing — WebSocket connections are created automatically

questions.q: It is guaranteed that you will always read the entire HTTP request after a single recv() call.
Which is NOT a way to process media in this class?
options: Using ffmpeg with an internal Python library
Using ffmpeg with Python bindings via an external library

questions.q: What is the most reliable way to determine the type of a file?
options: Assume the type based on the upload path
All files are just bytes — type never matters
Read the first bytes of the file to check its magic number / file signature
Check the Content-Type header sent by the client

questions.q: Which cookie directive prevents JavaScript from reading a cookie?
Which of the following is NOT true about multipart/form-data HTTP requests?
options: We receive them when an HTML form uses enctype='multipart/form-data'
This format is required for file uploads via HTML form submissions
The entire request can always be decoded and parsed as a UTF-8 string
Each part is separated by a boundary declared in Content-Type
Each form field is contained in a different part with its own headers

questions.q: Why do we use cookies in web apps? Explain the limitation of HTTP that makes cookies necessary.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Explicitly state that HTTP is stateless, so each request is handled independently.
[1 pt] State that cookies give the browser/server a way to preserve or re-send state across requests.
[1 pt] Give a concrete example such as authentication, remembering a session, or counting visits.

questions.q: Explain the difference between the `Set-Cookie` response header and the `Cookie` request header.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] `Set-Cookie` is sent by the server in a response to tell the browser to store a cookie.
[1 pt] `Cookie` is sent by the browser in later requests to return previously stored cookies.
[1 pt] Mention the one-cookie-per-Set-Cookie rule or that the browser combines multiple cookies in one Cookie header.

questions.q: Differentiate between session cookies and persistent cookies.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1.5 pts] Session cookies are deleted when the browser session ends and do not have Expires or Max-Age.
[1.5 pts] Persistent cookies survive browser restarts and are controlled by Expires or Max-Age.

questions.q: How do the `HttpOnly` and `Secure` flags improve cookie security?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1.5 pts] HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie, defending against session theft via XSS.
[1.5 pts] Secure restricts the cookie to HTTPS requests, reducing exposure over insecure transport.

questions.q: Write a fully formatted HTTP/1.1 POST request from a plain HTML form submitting `username=bob&password=pass123` to `/login` on `localhost:8080`. Include the required headers for the body.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Correct request line and Host header: POST /login HTTP/1.1 and Host: localhost:8080.
[1 pt] Includes Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded, Content-Length: 29, followed by \r\n\r\n, then the exact body username=bob&password=pass123.

questions.q: Write a fully formatted HTTP/1.1 response that redirects the browser to `/` and sets a persistent auth cookie named `auth_token`.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Correct redirect status and header: HTTP/1.1 302 Found and Location: /.
[1 pt] Includes Set-Cookie: auth_token=<value>; Max-Age=<positive>; HttpOnly, followed by \r\n\r\n.

questions.q: Explain how cookies add state to an otherwise stateless HTTP system. Your answer should include `Set-Cookie`, `Cookie`, session vs persistent cookies, and at least one security concern or directive.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] Explain that HTTP is stateless and cookies are used to attach state across requests.
[1 pt] Mention the flow: the server sends Set-Cookie, and the browser later sends Cookie back.
[1 pt] Differentiate session cookies from persistent cookies using Expires or Max-Age.
[1 pt] Include at least one security concern or directive such as HttpOnly, Secure, XSS, or CSRF.

questions.q: Explain what AJAX / `fetch` gives a web application and how it still fits into the normal HTTP request/response model.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] State that AJAX/fetch allows the browser to make HTTP requests after the page has already loaded.
[1 pt] Mention that this can happen without a full page reload, enabling more dynamic interfaces.
[1 pt] Explain that these are still ordinary HTTP requests with methods, headers, and optional bodies.
[1 pt] Give a practical example such as loading chat messages or submitting data asynchronously.

questions.q: Read this Python server snippet carefully. There are multiple bugs and security vulnerabilities. Find at least 3 issues for full credit. ```python # POST /api/chats — create a chat message def handle_create_chat(request, handler): data = json.loads(request.body) content = data["content"] author = "Guest" if "session" in request.cookies: doc = db.users.find_one({"session": request.cookies["session"]}) if doc: author = doc["username"] message = { "id": str(uuid.uuid4()), "author": author, "content": content, "updated": False } db.chats.insert_one(message) response = Response() response.text("" + content + "") handler.send_all(response.to_data()) ```
questions.rubric:
8 Points Total. Award up to 8 points for clearly identifying at least 3 of the following issues:

Security findings:
1. [XSS] The content is reflected directly back in an HTML <b> tag without escaping. A user who submits `<script>alert(1)</script>` as content will have it executed in other users' browsers.
2. [Missing session validation] The session cookie value is used as a direct MongoDB query field without hashing or any integrity check. An attacker who guesses or obtains a session ID gains full access.
3. [No Content-Type set] The response body contains HTML but no Content-Type: text/html header is set. Combined with MIME sniffing, this can cause unexpected rendering.

Bug findings:
4. [No input validation] The code does not check whether 'content' exists in data or whether the body is even valid JSON. A malformed request will raise an uncaught exception.
5. [No new session cookie issued] If the user does not have a session cookie, no cookie is set in the response. The spec requires the server to set a session cookie when a guest sends their first message.
6. [String concatenation for HTML] Using string concatenation to build an HTML response is fragile and error-prone — it bypasses any content-length calculation if to_data() measures bytes vs characters.

title: CSE 312 Mock Midterm — Variant B
description: Emphasizes authentication flows, token security, and multipart parsing from HW2–HW3.

questions.q: When implementing bcrypt to hash passwords, which of the following is true?
options: Bcrypt produces a deterministic hash for the same input every time
The salt is embedded in the bcrypt output string, so you do not need to store it separately
Bcrypt is a symmetric encryption algorithm
Bcrypt cannot be used with Python
Bcrypt hashes should be stored after a second SHA-256 pass

questions.q: Which HTTP status code should be returned when a user tries to delete another user's chat message?
Which of the following is true about auth tokens in HW2?
options: The plaintext token is stored in MongoDB
A salted hash of the token is stored in MongoDB
An unsalted hash of the token is stored in MongoDB
The token is stored in a file on disk

questions.q: What does the `HttpOnly` directive on a cookie prevent?
options: The cookie from being sent over HTTP (non-HTTPS) connections
The server from reading the cookie
JavaScript running on the page from accessing the cookie
The browser from storing the cookie

questions.q: In the multipart/form-data format, what separates each part of the body?
options: A boundary string prefixed with -- declared in the Content-Type header

questions.q: What is percent-encoding used for in HTTP?
options: Safely encoding special characters in URLs and form data

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the purpose of the `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` header?
options: Prevents the browser from guessing the MIME type and instead trusts the declared Content-Type
Prevents JavaScript from reading response headers

questions.q: When parsing a multipart body, the content of a part must be treated as:
options: Raw bytes, since binary files may be embedded

questions.q: What is password salting, and why is it important even when using a strong hashing algorithm?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] A salt is a unique random value added to each password before hashing.
[1 pt] It ensures two users with the same password produce different hashes, preventing rainbow-table attacks.
[1 pt] Without salting, an attacker who obtains the hash database can precompute hashes for common passwords and look them up instantly.

questions.q: Explain why auth tokens must be hashed before being stored in the database, even though they are not passwords.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] If the database is breached, storing plaintext tokens gives the attacker immediate access to all active sessions.
[1 pt] A hash means the attacker only obtains the hash, not the token, so they cannot directly replay it.
[1 pt] No salt is required because tokens are already high-entropy random values, unlike passwords.

questions.q: What is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting), and how does HTML escaping prevent it in a chat app?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] XSS is when a malicious user injects script tags or HTML into content that is then rendered by other users' browsers.
[1 pt] The injected script runs in the context of the page and can steal cookies, redirect users, or modify page content.
[1 pt] HTML escaping converts characters like < > & into their safe entity equivalents so they display as text rather than being parsed as HTML.

questions.q: Explain what happens during the WebSocket upgrade handshake at the HTTP level.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] The client sends an HTTP GET request with Upgrade: websocket and Connection: Upgrade headers, plus a Sec-WebSocket-Key.
[1 pt] The server responds with 101 Switching Protocols, Upgrade: websocket, and Sec-WebSocket-Accept derived from the key.
[1 pt] After the handshake, the same TCP connection is reused for bidirectional WebSocket frame exchange — no new connection is opened.

questions.q: Write the HTTP/1.1 request line, headers, and body for a PATCH request sent to `/api/chats/42` with JSON body `{"content": "edited"}` from a logged-in user with auth token `tok123`.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Correct request line: PATCH /api/chats/42 HTTP/1.1, plus Content-Type: application/json and Content-Length: 20.
[1 pt] Cookie: auth_token=tok123 header present, followed by \r\n\r\n and body {"content": "edited"}.

questions.q: Write the HTTP/1.1 response your server sends when a user successfully registers. Include the status line and at minimum two relevant headers.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Status line: HTTP/1.1 200 OK.
[1 pt] Includes Content-Type and Content-Length (or Transfer-Encoding) headers, and optionally X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Body may be a short confirmation string.

questions.q: Explain the full login flow from the moment a user submits the login form to the moment they can access a protected resource. Include credential validation, token issuance, and cookie behavior.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] The browser sends a POST request with url-encoded username and password in the body.
[1 pt] The server looks up the user, hashes the provided password, and compares against the stored bcrypt hash.
[1 pt] On success, the server generates a high-entropy auth token, stores a hash of it in the DB, and sends it as an HttpOnly persistent cookie.
[1 pt] On subsequent requests the browser sends Cookie: auth_token=<token>; the server hashes the token and compares with the DB entry to authenticate.

questions.q: Explain why the Content-Length header is critical in HTTP and what problems arise if it is calculated incorrectly.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] Content-Length tells the receiver exactly how many bytes constitute the body of the message.
[1 pt] Without it, the receiver has no way to know when the body ends, causing hangs or truncation.
[1 pt] If calculated on Python str length rather than bytes length, multi-byte UTF-8 characters (e.g. emoji) will produce an incorrect value.
[1 pt] An incorrect value can cause the browser to truncate content, display garbage, or break keep-alive pipelining.

questions.q:
Read this Python login handler carefully. There are multiple bugs and security vulnerabilities. Find at least 3 issues for full credit.

```python
# POST /login
def handle_login(request, handler):
    body = request.body.decode()
    parts = body.split("&")
    username = parts[0].split("=")[1]
    password = parts[1].split("=")[1]

    user = db.users.find_one({"username": username})

    if user and user["password"] == password:
        token = str(uuid.uuid4())
        db.users.update_one(
            {"username": username},
            {"$set": {"auth_token": token}}
        )
        response = Response()
        response.set_status(200, "OK")
        response.cookies({"auth_token": token})
        handler.send_all(response.to_data())
    else:
        response = Response()
        response.set_status(400, "Bad Request")
        response.text("Login failed")
        handler.send_all(response.to_data())
```
questions.rubric:
8 Points Total. Award up to 8 points for clearly identifying at least 3 of the following issues:

Security findings:
1. [Plaintext password comparison] The code compares user["password"] == password directly, meaning passwords are stored as plaintext in MongoDB. Correct approach: use bcrypt.checkpw().
2. [Auth token stored in plaintext] The raw token string is stored in MongoDB. A database breach reveals all active tokens. The token hash (no salt needed) should be stored instead.
3. [Missing HttpOnly directive] The auth_token cookie is set without the HttpOnly directive, allowing JavaScript to steal the cookie via XSS.

Bug findings:
4. [Fragile URL decoding] The manual split on '&' and '=' does not handle percent-encoded characters. A password like 'p%40ss' will not be decoded correctly and login will fail even with the correct password.
5. [No validation that parts exist] If the body is malformed (e.g. missing '&'), indexing parts[1] will raise an IndexError and crash the server.
6. [Session cookie not persistent] No Max-Age or Expires directive is set, so the cookie is a session cookie that disappears when the browser is closed, contradicting the HW2 spec.

title: CSE 312 Mock Midterm — Variant C
description: Focuses on routing logic, static file serving, path traversal, and HTTP security headers from HW1.

questions.q: In HW1, what does the `exact_path=True` flag on `add_route` mean?
options: The request path must begin with the route path
The request path must match the route path exactly
The route only accepts GET requests
The route applies to all HTTP methods

questions.q: Which of the following Content-Type values should be used when serving a .mp4 file?
What security header must be sent on every response to prevent browsers from guessing file types?
What is the correct response status code for a resource that cannot be found?
When rendering a page using layout.html and a content file in HW1, what string is replaced?
Which HTTP method is used to partially update a resource (e.g., edit a chat message)?
Which Python class is the correct return type of parse_multipart() in HW3?
options: A dict with boundary and parts keys
An object with boundary and parts fields (from a custom class)

questions.q: In HTTP, which header tells the browser how many bytes to read for the response body?
What is a path traversal attack, and how should a file-serving endpoint guard against it?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Path traversal is when an attacker uses sequences like ../../ in a URL to read files outside the intended directory.
[1 pt] An attacker could access /etc/passwd or server configuration files by requesting /public/../../etc/passwd.
[1 pt] Guard against it by resolving the full path with os.path.realpath and verifying it starts with the expected base directory before opening the file.

questions.q: Explain why the body of an HTTP request must be handled as bytes rather than decoded to a string immediately.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Binary files (images, videos) cannot be decoded as UTF-8 — decoding them will corrupt or raise an error.
[1 pt] Decoding the entire request before splitting headers/body can corrupt binary bodies if a byte sequence is not valid UTF-8.
[1 pt] The correct approach is to find the first b'\r\n\r\n' in bytes, treat everything after as the body bytes, and only decode the headers portion.

questions.q: What are the three parts of an HTTP/1.1 request line, and what does each contain?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] The HTTP method (e.g. GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE).
[1 pt] The request path/URL (e.g. /api/chats or /public/style.css).
[1 pt] The HTTP version string (always HTTP/1.1 in this course), each part separated by a space.

questions.q: Explain the difference between encoding and encryption. Give one example of each in the context of web development.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Encoding transforms data into a different format for transmission/storage but is reversible without a key (e.g. base64, percent-encoding, UTF-8).
[1 pt] Encryption transforms data so it can only be reversed with a secret key (e.g. AES, TLS).
[1 pt] A strong example of each: percent-encoding of a URL (encoding) vs. HTTPS/TLS protecting data in transit (encryption).

questions.q: Write the full HTTP/1.1 response your server sends when serving `public/imgs/logo.png`. Include the correct status line and all required headers.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Status line: HTTP/1.1 200 OK, with Content-Type: image/png.
[1 pt] Includes Content-Length equal to the number of bytes in the file, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, and the binary body after \r\n\r\n.

questions.q: Write the full HTTP/1.1 response for a 404 error when a client requests `/api/does-not-exist`.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Status line: HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found.
[1 pt] Includes Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8 and Content-Length with a plain-text body explaining the resource was not found.

questions.q: Explain how TCP streaming affects HTTP request parsing in Python. What can go wrong with a naive implementation, and how do you fix it?
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] TCP is a stream protocol — data may arrive in arbitrary chunks, not necessarily one full request per recv().
[1 pt] A naive recv(4096) may return only part of the HTTP request; constructing a Request object from it will fail or produce corrupt data.
[1 pt] For the headers: keep buffering until \r\n\r\n is seen. Then read Content-Length additional bytes into the body.
[1 pt] Failure to buffer properly causes silent data loss, crashes on large uploads, and security issues if an attacker sends a slow request.

questions.q: Explain how the Router class works in HW1, including how exact_path and route ordering affect which handler is called.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] Routes are registered with add_route(method, path, handler, exact_path=False).
[1 pt] When a request arrives, route_request iterates through registered routes to find the first match.
[1 pt] If exact_path=True, the request path must equal the route path exactly. If False, the request path must start with the route path.
[1 pt] The first matching route wins, so more specific routes should be registered before broader prefix routes to avoid being shadowed.

questions.q:
Read this static file-serving handler carefully. There are multiple bugs and security vulnerabilities. Find at least 3 issues for full credit.

```python
# Handles GET /public/<anything>
def serve_file(request, handler):
    path = request.path  # e.g. /public/imgs/dog.jpg
    file_path = "." + path  # => ./public/imgs/dog.jpg

    with open(file_path, "rb") as f:
        content = f.read()

    ext = path.split(".")[-1]
    mime_types = {
        "html": "text/html",
        "css":  "text/css",
        "js":   "application/javascript",
        "png":  "image/png",
        "jpg":  "image/jpg"
    }
    content_type = mime_types.get(ext, "text/plain")

    response = Response()
    response.headers({"Content-Type": content_type})
    response.bytes(content)
    handler.send_all(response.to_data())
```
questions.rubric:
8 Points Total. Award up to 8 points for clearly identifying at least 3 of the following issues:

Security findings:
1. [Path Traversal] The path is concatenated directly without validation. A request for /public/../../etc/passwd will open a file outside the public directory. Fix: use os.path.realpath and verify the resolved path starts with the public directory.
2. [Wrong MIME type for JPEG] The MIME type for .jpg is image/jpeg, not image/jpg. Using the wrong type can cause browsers to mishandle the file and defeats X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff.

Bug findings:
3. [Missing X-Content-Type-Options header] No nosniff header is set on any response. This is required as a security measure per the HW1 spec.
4. [No 404 handling] If the file does not exist, open() raises a FileNotFoundError and the server crashes instead of returning a 404.
5. [Missing MIME types] .gif, .ico, .mp4 and other extensions present in the public directory are not in the mime_types dict and will be served as text/plain, which breaks rendering.
6. [Incorrect ext split on slashes] If the path has no extension (e.g. /public/somedir), split('.')[-1] returns the last segment without a dot, and the fallback text/plain is silently used with no error.

title: CSE 312 Mock Midterm — Variant D
description: Focuses on multipart parsing, avatar uploads, video endpoints, and binary data handling from HW3.

questions.q: In HW3, why must the multipart body content be stored as bytes rather than a decoded string?
options: Binary files like images and videos cannot be decoded as UTF-8
Strings take more memory than bytes

questions.q: When parsing a multipart body, the boundary in the body is prefixed with:
Which of the following is the correct MIME type for a .gif file?
When a user uploads a second avatar image, what should happen to the first one per the HW3 spec?
options: Both images are kept and the user picks which to display
The first image is deleted from disk
The first is replaced — the new upload should overwrite or replace the old entry
The server stores all versions for history

questions.q: What does the `created_at` field in the GET /api/videos response contain?
options: A timestamp string indicating when the video was uploaded

questions.q: Why should uploaded files be saved to disk rather than stored directly in MongoDB?
options: MongoDB does not support binary data
Disk storage is faster for all use cases
Large binary files are expensive to store and transfer in a database; disk is more appropriate
Files stored in MongoDB cannot be deleted
The HW spec requires disk because Docker does not support MongoDB blobs

questions.q: Which Python library is recommended in HW3 for generating video thumbnails from specific timestamps?
What HTTP method is used to change a video's thumbnail in HW3 AO1?
Explain what buffering is in the context of receiving HTTP requests, and why a simple `recv(4096)` is not sufficient for large uploads.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] TCP delivers data as a stream; a single recv() may not return the entire request body.
[1 pt] For large uploads (images, videos), the body can be megabytes or gigabytes — a single small buffer will miss data.
[1 pt] Correct approach: read Content-Length from the headers, then loop calling recv() and accumulating bytes until the full body is received.

questions.q: Explain the structure of a multipart/form-data request body at the byte level.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] The body starts with --<boundary> on its own line, followed by headers for the first part (e.g. Content-Disposition, Content-Type), then a blank line, then the part content.
[1 pt] Each additional part is introduced by another --<boundary> line.
[1 pt] The body ends with --<boundary>-- (note the trailing --).

questions.q: What is Adaptive Bit-Rate (ABR) streaming, and how does HLS implement it?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] ABR lets the player switch between different quality levels of a video based on available bandwidth.
[1 pt] HLS uses .m3u8 playlist files: a master playlist references multiple stream playlists at different resolutions/bitrates.
[1 pt] Each stream playlist lists .ts (transport stream) segment files; the player downloads segments from the appropriate quality stream.

questions.q: Describe two ways a server can identify the type of an uploaded file and explain which is more reliable.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Method 1: check the file extension from the filename submitted by the client.
[1 pt] Method 2: read the first few bytes of the file and match against known file signatures (magic bytes), e.g. \xFF\xD8\xFF for JPEG.
[1 pt] Magic bytes are more reliable because an attacker can rename any file to .jpg; the magic bytes reveal the true format. (For this course checking the extension is acceptable, but not more reliable.)

questions.q: Write the HTTP/1.1 response your server returns after a user successfully uploads a video. The response must include the generated video ID.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Status line: HTTP/1.1 200 OK, with Content-Type: application/json.
[1 pt] Body is a JSON object with an id field: {"id": "<generated-uuid>"}, and Content-Length reflects the byte length of the JSON body.

questions.q: Sketch the structure of a multipart request body (just the text portions — you don't need real binary data) for an avatar upload where the form field is named `avatar` and the file is `photo.png`.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Shows the boundary line --<boundary>, then Content-Disposition: form-data; name="avatar"; filename="photo.png" header.
[1 pt] Followed by a blank line (\r\n), then the imagined binary content, then ending with --<boundary>--.

questions.q: Explain the full lifecycle of a video upload in HW3: from the multipart request arriving at the server to the video being playable at GET /api/videos/{id}.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] Server receives a multipart POST /api/videos request; parse_multipart() extracts the title, description, and video bytes.
[1 pt] The video bytes are written to a file on disk (e.g. in public/videos/) with a generated unique name.
[1 pt] A MongoDB document is inserted with title, description, file path, author_id, created_at, and generated id.
[1 pt] GET /api/videos/{id} queries MongoDB by id, finds the document, and returns the metadata as JSON; the video_path tells the client where to fetch the actual file.

questions.q: Explain what TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) is and how a server verifies a TOTP code without storing the code in the database.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] TOTP is an algorithm that generates a short-lived numeric code based on a shared secret and the current time.
[1 pt] The server stores only the user's TOTP secret (not the code). The client app (e.g. Google Authenticator) uses the same secret + current time to generate the same code.
[1 pt] When the user submits a code, the server runs the TOTP algorithm with the stored secret and current time and checks if its output matches the submitted code.
[1 pt] Codes are typically valid for 30 seconds and expire automatically, making replay attacks impractical.

questions.q:
Read this avatar upload handler carefully. There are multiple bugs and security vulnerabilities. Find at least 3 issues for full credit.

```python
# POST /api/users/avatar
def handle_avatar_upload(request, handler):
    result = parse_multipart(request)
    avatar_part = result.parts[0]

    filename = avatar_part.headers["Content-Disposition"].split("filename=\"")[1].rstrip('"')
    save_path = "public/imgs/profile-pics/" + filename

    with open(save_path, "wb") as f:
        f.write(avatar_part.content)

    db.users.update_one(
        {"session": request.cookies.get("session")},
        {"$set": {"imageURL": "/" + save_path}}
    )

    response = Response()
    response.set_status(200, "OK")
    response.text("Avatar updated")
    handler.send_all(response.to_data())
```
questions.rubric:
8 Points Total. Award up to 8 points for clearly identifying at least 3 of the following issues:

Security findings:
1. [Path Traversal via filename] The client-supplied filename is used directly to build the save path. An attacker can upload a file named ../../server.py and overwrite server source files.
2. [No auth check] The code looks up the user by session cookie without verifying the session is valid or that the user is logged in via auth_token. Any request with any session value can update a user record.
3. [No file type validation] The code saves any uploaded content with the client-supplied extension. An attacker could upload a .py or .html file that gets served as a script or page.

Bug findings:
4. [Auth lookup uses wrong field] HW2 identifies users by auth_token (hashed), not by a session cookie. Querying by 'session' will fail to find the user after HW2 authentication is implemented.
5. [Filename fragile parsing] If the Content-Disposition header does not contain filename=" (e.g. the filename contains special chars or is missing), the split will raise an IndexError.
6. [No response Content-Length check] The response is sent without verifying to_data() produces a valid response; if the Response class requires at least bytes() or text() to be called first, this might produce an empty body.

title: CSE 312 Mock Midterm — Variant E
description: Mixed review of HTTP, WebSockets, emoji reactions, 2FA, and OAuth 2.0 from HW1–HW2 AOs.

questions.q: When a client sends a WebSocket frame, the payload is:
options: Always masked using a 4-byte masking key with XOR

questions.q: The WebSocket frame header contains which of the following? (Select the most complete correct answer)
options: FIN bit, opcode, MASK bit, payload length
Only the payload length and CRC checksum

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the Authorization Code Flow in OAuth 2.0?
options: The client receives the access token directly from the authorization server without a redirect
The user's password is sent to the client app, which exchanges it for a token
The browser is redirected to the auth server, user approves, a code is sent to the redirect URI, and the server exchanges the code for a token
The client generates a token locally using the client secret
The token is embedded in the URL fragment and never reaches the server

questions.q: In TOTP-based 2FA, what two inputs are combined to generate a one-time code?
options: The user's password and a random salt
A shared secret and the current timestamp (rounded to 30-second intervals)
The username and the server's private key
A random nonce and the user's email
The HMAC of the session token and the client IP

questions.q: The `Sec-WebSocket-Accept` header in the 101 response is derived from:
options: The client's IP address and port
A SHA-1 hash of the Sec-WebSocket-Key concatenated with a GUID, then base64-encoded
An HMAC of the path and method
A random token generated by the server

questions.q: In HW1, when should a new session cookie be issued to a chat user?
options: When they first load the homepage
When they first send a chat message
Only when they explicitly request one via /api/session

questions.q: For emoji reactions in HW2 AO1, what should the server return if a user tries to react to the same message with the same emoji twice?
Which OAuth 2.0 concern makes it important to validate the `state` parameter in the callback?
options: It ensures the access token is not expired
It prevents CSRF attacks on the OAuth flow
It verifies the user's email address
It confirms the scopes granted match what was requested
It ensures the redirect URI is HTTPS

questions.q: Explain the three-step process for XOR-unmasking a WebSocket payload in Python.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Extract the 4-byte masking key from bytes 2–5 (or wherever the key appears based on payload length).
[1 pt] For each byte i in the payload, compute unmasked[i] = payload[i] XOR masking_key[i % 4].
[1 pt] The result is the decoded payload bytes, which can then be decoded as UTF-8 for text frames.

questions.q: Explain the security risk of storing a GitHub OAuth client secret in your source code and how the HW2 spec requires you to handle it.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] If the client secret is committed to a public repository, attackers can use it to impersonate your app and obtain OAuth tokens for any user who connects.
[1 pt] The HW2 spec requires storing secrets in a .env file (not committed to the repo) with placeholder values in submissions.
[1 pt] At runtime, python-dotenv (or os.environ) loads the values, keeping secrets out of source code and version control.

questions.q: Explain what percent-encoding is and give an example of how a form password like `p@ss#1` would be encoded in an x-www-form-urlencoded body.
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] Percent-encoding replaces special characters with % followed by two hex digits representing the ASCII code.
[1 pt] The @ character is %40 and # is %23.
[1 pt] So p@ss#1 becomes p%40ss%231 in the encoded body.

questions.q: Why is it important that the `state` parameter in OAuth 2.0 be a randomly generated, unguessable value?
questions.rubric:
3 Points Total:
[1 pt] State is sent to the authorization server and echoed back in the redirect to your callback URL.
[1 pt] If state is predictable, an attacker can craft a malicious link that completes the OAuth flow on behalf of a victim, linking the attacker's GitHub account to the victim's session (CSRF on OAuth).
[1 pt] A random, session-bound state ties the callback to the specific user who initiated the flow, preventing this attack.

questions.q: Write the full HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols response your server sends to upgrade a connection to WebSocket. Include all required headers.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] Status line: HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols.
[1 pt] Headers: Upgrade: websocket, Connection: Upgrade, Sec-WebSocket-Accept: <base64-sha1-hash>, followed by \r\n\r\n with no body.

questions.q: Write the HTTP/1.1 request your server sends to GitHub's token endpoint to exchange an authorization code for an access token. List the method, URL, and required body parameters.
questions.rubric:
2 Points Total:
[1 pt] POST https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token with Accept: application/json and Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded.
[1 pt] Body must contain client_id, client_secret, code (the authorization code), and redirect_uri.

questions.q: Explain the full OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow for the 'Login with GitHub' feature in HW2, starting from the user clicking the button to the user being logged in.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] GET /authgithub: server redirects browser to GitHub's authorization URL with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and a random state.
[1 pt] User approves on GitHub; GitHub redirects to /authcallback with code and state. Server validates state matches the one it sent.
[1 pt] Server makes a server-side POST to GitHub's token endpoint with client_id, client_secret, and code to obtain an access token.
[1 pt] Server uses the access token to call GitHub's user API, retrieves the username, creates or looks up the user in MongoDB, issues an auth_token cookie, and redirects to the homepage.

questions.q: Explain how WebSocket enables real-time bidirectional communication compared to standard HTTP polling.
questions.rubric:
4 Points Total:
[1 pt] HTTP polling requires the client to repeatedly send requests to check for new data, introducing latency and wasting bandwidth.
[1 pt] WebSocket reuses the same TCP connection after the upgrade handshake; either side can push data at any time.
[1 pt] The server can push a new chat message to all connected clients immediately without them asking.
[1 pt] Frames are small (compared to full HTTP headers on each poll request), making WebSocket more efficient for high-frequency updates like chat or live reactions.

questions.q:
Read this emoji-reaction handler carefully. There are multiple bugs and security vulnerabilities. Find at least 3 issues for full credit.

```python
# PATCH /api/reaction/<message_id>
def handle_add_reaction(request, handler):
    message_id = request.path.split("/")[-1]
    data = json.loads(request.body)
    emoji = data["emoji"]

    auth = request.cookies.get("auth_token")
    user = db.users.find_one({"auth_token": auth})

    if not user:
        response = Response()
        response.set_status(403, "Forbidden")
        handler.send_all(response.to_data())
        return

    msg = db.chats.find_one({"id": message_id})
    reactions = msg.get("reactions", {})

    if emoji in reactions and user["id"] in reactions[emoji]:
        reactions[emoji].append(user["id"])
    else:
        reactions[emoji] = [user["id"]]

    db.chats.update_one({"id": message_id}, {"$set": {"reactions": reactions}})

    response = Response()
    response.set_status(200, "OK")
    response.text("Reaction added")
    handler.send_all(response.to_data())
```
questions.rubric:
8 Points Total. Award up to 8 points for clearly identifying at least 3 of the following issues:

Security findings:
1. [Plaintext auth_token lookup] The code queries MongoDB with the raw auth_token cookie value. Per HW2, the DB should store a hash of the token. An attacker who reads the DB cannot reverse the hash — but this code stores and compares plaintext, negating that protection.
2. [Logic inversion on duplicate reaction] The condition `if emoji in reactions and user["id"] in reactions[emoji]` should trigger a 403 (duplicate reaction). Instead it calls append(), adding the user ID a second time. The else branch sets a new list, overwriting existing reactors. The duplicate check logic is completely backwards.

Bug findings:
3. [No 403 for duplicate] When the user has already reacted with the same emoji, the spec requires a 403. The code instead silently appends the duplicate and returns 200.
4. [No check if message exists] db.chats.find_one() can return None if the message_id doesn't match. Calling msg.get() on None raises an AttributeError and crashes the server.
5. [Emoji not validated] The emoji field is used directly as a MongoDB key. A malicious user sending an emoji value containing special characters (e.g. a MongoDB operator like $where) could alter query behavior.
6. [reactions dict overwrites on else branch] When reactions[emoji] doesn't exist yet, the else branch correctly creates a new list. But when the emoji exists and the user is NOT yet in the list, the condition's if branch appends (correct behavior) — however, the condition block also handles the duplicate case incorrectly as described above, so real first-addition and duplicate-addition are confused.
